og.title: Marriage Contracts vs Cohabitation Agreements in Ontario
og.description: Plain-language Ontario guidance comparing marriage contracts (prenups/postnups) and cohabitation agreements—what they do, how they differ, and which one you need.

keywords: marriage contract vs cohabitation agreement Ontario
common law vs married property Ontario
which agreement do I need Ontario

modules.title: Marriage Contracts vs Cohabitation Agreements in Ontario
modules.subtitle: Ontario law treats married and unmarried couples very differently. Marriage contracts and cohabitation agreements serve similar goals—but apply in different legal contexts. Our Separation helps you choose and create the right agreement for your relationship.

modules.title: Why the Distinction Matters in Ontario
body: Couples often use the terms "prenup," "cohabitation agreement," and "marriage contract" interchangeably. In Ontario law, these are not the same thing.
The agreement you need depends primarily on whether you are married or not. Ontario's Family Law Act creates very different default rules for married spouses and unmarried (common-law) partners.
Understanding this distinction is critical. Using the wrong type of agreement—or assuming one applies when it does not—can leave you exposed to significant financial risk.

left.heading: Marriage contracts
content: A marriage contract is an agreement between spouses or soon-to-be spouses.

• Prenuptial agreements (signed before marriage)
• Postnuptial agreements (signed after marriage)
They are used to modify or replace Ontario's default rules for married couples, particularly around property equalization and spousal support.

right.heading: Cohabitation agreements
content: A cohabitation agreement is an agreement between unmarried partners who live together or plan to live together.
Because Ontario law does not automatically divide property for common-law partners, cohabitation agreements often create structure where none exists by default.
If the couple later marries, a cohabitation agreement can automatically become a marriage contract.

rows.left: Applies only if the parties are married
rows.right: Applies only if the parties are not married
Creates property rules where none exist by default

rows.left: Equalization of net family property applies unless altered
rows.right: No automatic property sharing without an agreement

rows.left: Special statutory rules apply to the matrimonial home
Often used to protect premarital assets
rows.right: Often used to define ownership during cohabitation

modules.title: How Property Is Treated Differently
modules.subtitle: Property is the single biggest legal difference between married and unmarried couples in Ontario.

items.title: Married couples
items.description: Ontario law requires equalization of net family property on separation, regardless of whose name assets are in—subject to limited exclusions.

items.title: Common-law couples
items.description: There is no automatic right to share property. Ownership is determined by title, trust claims, or agreement.

items.title: Why agreements matter
items.description: Marriage contracts can limit or alter equalization. Cohabitation agreements can create predictability where the law otherwise provides none.

items.title: Marriage contracts
items.description: Can include spousal support waivers, limits, or formulas, subject to fairness and disclosure.

items.title: Cohabitation agreements
items.description: Can also address spousal support, even though property rights differ for common-law partners.

items.title: Important limitation
items.description: Neither type of agreement can predetermine child support or parenting arrangements.

cards.title: You are planning to marry
cards.body: You need a prenuptial agreement (marriage contract).

cards.title: You are already married
cards.body: You need a postnuptial agreement (marriage contract after marriage).

cards.title: You live together but are not married
You may marry in the future
cards.body: A cohabitation agreement can later convert into a marriage contract.

link.label: Plan ahead with a cohabitation agreement

items.q: Is a cohabitation agreement the same as a prenup?
items.a: No. A cohabitation agreement applies to unmarried couples. A prenup is a marriage contract that applies once the couple marries.

items.q: What happens if we sign a cohabitation agreement and later marry?
items.a: In Ontario, a cohabitation agreement can automatically become a marriage contract if it meets legal requirements.

items.q: Do both agreements need to be in writing?
items.a: Yes. Both marriage contracts and cohabitation agreements must be in writing, signed, and witnessed.

items.q: Can either agreement deal with children?
items.a: No. Parenting arrangements and child support cannot be predetermined in these agreements.
Neither is inherently stronger. The right agreement depends on whether you are married and what risks you are trying to manage.

modules.title: Choose the Right Agreement—With Confidence
modules.subtitle: Marriage contracts and cohabitation agreements solve different problems. Our Separation helps you choose and create the right one for your relationship.

mainEntity.name: Is a cohabitation agreement the same as a prenup?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. A cohabitation agreement applies to unmarried couples, while a prenup is a marriage contract that applies once the couple marries.

mainEntity.name: What happens if we sign a cohabitation agreement and later marry?

acceptedAnswer.text: In Ontario, a cohabitation agreement can automatically become a marriage contract if it meets legal requirements.

mainEntity.name: Can these agreements deal with children?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Parenting arrangements and child support cannot be predetermined in marriage contracts or cohabitation agreements.
